text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Patient: Renata Okonkwo
Attending: Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine follow-up, metastatic melanoma on nivolumab maintenance. Here today to review recent CT results and discuss ongoing treatment plan.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo is a 56-year-old woman with Stage IV (M1c) malignant melanoma of the trunk, BRAF V600E-mutated, who presents for her scheduled follow-up visit. She is approximately 18 months into her overall immunotherapy course and 14 months into nivolumab (Opdivo) maintenance following completion of the induction phase.

To briefly recap her trajectory: she was diagnosed with widely metastatic melanoma involving bilateral axillary and inguinal lymph nodes, multiple subcutaneous nodules, and hepatic metastases. She was initiated on dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg BID plus trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg daily as first-line therapy given her BRAF V600E positivity and high hepatic disease burden requiring rapid cytoreduction. She had a striking initial response — nodal disease and liver lesions shrank markedly within 2 months — but she progressed at approximately 8 months, with emergence of new cervical nodal disease, new hepatic lesions, and a peritoneal nodule. Liquid biopsy at that time demonstrated acquired MEK1 (MAP2K1) C121S resistance mutation and BRAF amplification, consistent with MAPK pathway reactivation.

She was transitioned to second-line ipilimumab (Yervoy) 3 mg/kg plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 1 mg/kg Q3W per the CheckMate-067 schema. She received 3 of 4 planned cycles before developing Grade 3 immune-related colitis requiring hospitalization, IV methylprednisolone, and ultimately infliximab for a steroid-refractory component. Ipi was permanently discontinued. Following an 8-week steroid taper and gastroenterology clearance, she resumed nivolumab 480 mg IV Q4W monotherapy approximately 16 weeks after the initial ipi/nivo start.

Since resuming nivolumab, she has had a gratifying and deepening response. At her 10-month immunotherapy mark the CT showed deep partial response with near-resolution of peritoneal disease and significant regression of hepatic lesions. Her most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (reviewed and discussed today, obtained approximately 3 weeks ago at roughly 15 months into immunotherapy) showed stable disease with no progression: the right cervical lymph node is 0.5 cm, right axillary node 0.6 cm, and two residual hepatic lesions both 0.4 cm — all stable from the prior scan. LDH was 148 U/L, well within normal range. No new lesions identified.

Today she reports feeling very well. She has returned to full-time work as a landscape architect and is quite physically active, spending long hours outdoors. She has had no new or recurring symptoms attributable to her melanoma — no new skin nodules, no lymph node swelling, no abdominal discomfort. She specifically denies: new rash, diarrhea, shortness of breath, dyspnea on exertion, visual changes, joint pains, or headaches. She reports mild, intermittent fatigue in the late afternoons, which she attributes to her workload and which has been stable for months.

With respect to immune-related toxicities: colitis is in full clinical remission confirmed by GI at her most recent gastroenterology follow-up approximately 2 months ago — she has been completely off steroids for many months and her stool frequency is back to baseline 1-2 times per day with no cramping or urgency. She continues on levothyroxine 112 mcg daily for her combined pre-existing and nivolumab-exacerbated hypothyroidism; her TSH has been well-controlled. She denies adrenal insufficiency symptoms. No arthralgias beyond baseline.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild late-afternoon fatigue, stable. No fever, no unintentional weight loss. Appetite good.
Skin: No new pigmented lesions. No new skin nodules. No rash. Most recent dermatology visit approximately 6 weeks ago — no concerning lesions, no new SCC or other keratotic lesions.
Eyes: No blurred vision, no photophobia. Prior history of anterior uveitis remote and quiescent.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.
Pulmonary: No cough, dyspnea, or pleuritic chest pain. No wheezing.
Gastrointestinal: Bowel movements 1-2x/day, formed, no blood, no mucus, no cramping. Good appetite and oral intake.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria, or foamy urine.
Musculoskeletal: No new joint swelling or inflammatory arthralgias. Mild nonspecific lower back discomfort after prolonged standing at work — chronic and unchanged.
Neurological: No headache, cognitive symptoms, or focal neurological deficits.
Endocrine: On levothyroxine, feels euthyroid. No cold intolerance, hair loss, or palpitations.
Hematologic: No bleeding or bruising.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing, athletic-framed woman in no distress. Engaged and communicative.
Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 64 bpm, RR 14, Temp 98.4 F, SpO2 99% on room air. Weight 68 kg (stable from last visit).
Skin: No palpable subcutaneous nodules over trunk or extremities. No maculopapular eruption. No keratoacanthoma or SCC. Scar from primary excision on mid-back well healed. Prior SCC excision sites (left forearm, right shin) well healed with no recurrence.
Lymphadenopathy: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy. Inguinal regions without palpable adenopathy. Right axillary region: soft, no distinct nodularity on palpation (consistent with residual small-volume disease on imaging).
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: PERRL, EOMI, no scleral icterus. Oropharynx clear, no mucosal lesions.
Neck: Supple, no palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. No thyromegaly.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, S1/S2 without murmurs. No peripheral edema.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheezing, or rubs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. No peritoneal signs. Bowel sounds active.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CBC: WBC 6.1 K/uL (differential within normal limits), Hgb 13.8 g/dL, Plt 224 K/uL
CMP: Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.0 mEq/L, Cr 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR >60), BUN 14 mg/dL, glucose 94 mg/dL
Liver function: AST 28 U/L, ALT 31 U/L, total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 74 U/L, albumin 4.2 g/dL
LDH: 151 U/L (normal, ULN 210 U/L)
TSH: 2.8 mIU/L (normal on levothyroxine 112 mcg)
Urinalysis: negative protein, negative blood, no casts

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (obtained ~3 weeks prior): Reviewed in detail. As noted above, stable findings throughout with residual small bilateral axillary nodes (right 0.6 cm, left borderline), and two residual hepatic lesions both measuring 0.4 cm — stable from prior. No new lesions. No peritoneal disease. No pleural effusion or pulmonary nodules. Colon wall appearance normal. LDH confirmed normal at time of scan. Overall impression: continued stable partial response, no progression.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. METASTATIC MELANOMA, BRAF V600E+, Stage IV M1c — stable partial response on nivolumab maintenance, 18 months total immunotherapy / 14 months nivolumab monotherapy:

Ms. Okonkwo continues to do very well. She has maintained a durable partial response on Opdivo monotherapy following prior combination ipi/nivo (complicated by Grade 3 colitis, ipi discontinued). Her disease is stable — the residual sub-centimeter nodes and tiny hepatic foci have not grown and remain at or near the limit of CT detection. LDH is consistently normal, which is reassuring. Performance status excellent at ECOG 0.

We had a substantive discussion today regarding treatment duration. This is a nuanced and individualized decision. The emerging data from long-term follow-up of CheckMate-067 and the single-arm studies of treatment discontinuation suggest that patients who achieve deep responses after 2 years of immunotherapy — particularly those with complete responses — may safely stop therapy, with a meaningful proportion remaining progression-free off treatment. However, Ms. Okonkwo has not yet achieved a complete response by imaging criteria: the residual 0.4 cm hepatic foci remain visible on CT, even if metabolically they may be quiescent or representing scar. Given the gravity of her prior disease burden (M1c with hepatic and peritoneal spread) and the absence of documented complete response, I discussed the following options:
   a) Continue nivolumab for up to 24 months total immunotherapy and reassess.
   b) Consider PET-CT to better characterize metabolic activity of the residual hepatic foci — if metabolically inactive, this would support a discussion of discontinuation.

After discussion, the multidisciplinary team (reviewed at last week's melanoma tumor board) and I are recommending that she continue nivolumab at this time given ongoing — albeit stabilized — residual disease. She was enrolled today in a registry study tracking long-term outcomes on PD-1 maintenance immunotherapy after prior combination checkpoint blockade. I reviewed the registry protocol with her in detail; she consented and signed the registration paperwork.

Continue nivolumab (Opdivo) 480 mg IV Q4W. Next infusion scheduled in 4 weeks.

2. IMMUNE-RELATED HYPOTHYROIDISM (superimposed on pre-existing hypothyroidism):
TSH 2.8 mIU/L — well-controlled on levothyroxine 112 mcg daily. No dose adjustment needed. Continue quarterly TSH monitoring. Endocrinology sees her every 6 months.

3. PRIOR GRADE 3 IMMUNE-RELATED COLITIS — in complete remission:
No recurrent GI symptoms. Stool frequency normal. Off steroids. Gastroenterology has confirmed full endoscopic and clinical remission. She remains on nivolumab without ipi reintroduction. She knows to contact the clinic immediately with any increase in stool frequency, blood in stool, or abdominal pain.

4. DERMATOLOGIC SURVEILLANCE:
Most recent derm visit 6 weeks ago — no new cutaneous SCC or melanoma lesions. She is aware of the ongoing need for skin surveillance given prior history of BRAF-inhibitor-associated SCCs (now resolved) and underlying history of atypical nevi. Next dermatology visit in approximately 6 weeks.

5. HYPOTHYROIDISM (pre-existing, on levothyroxine):
Well-controlled as above. No changes.

6. REGISTRY ENROLLMENT:
Enrolled today in registry study. No protocol-required additional testing at this time beyond standard of care labs.

Return to clinic in 4 weeks at time of next infusion for interval labs. Repeat CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 3 months. Patient instructed to contact the clinic immediately for: new lymph node swelling, new skin lesions, increased stool frequency or diarrhea, shortness of breath, visual changes, or any other new symptoms. After-hours on-call line number reviewed with patient. She verbalized understanding.

Time spent with patient: approximately 45 minutes, including discussion of imaging results, treatment duration counseling, registry enrollment, and question-and-answer.

---

Electronically signed, Medical Oncology
